user_summary: Senior AI safety researcher with an empirical, practical orientation spanning alignment, evaluations, and governance. Their seed papers — AI Control (Greenblatt et al. 2023), Scalable Oversight (Bowman et al. 2022), and Constitutional AI (Bai et al. 2022) — point to a specific cluster: concrete alignment mechanisms for models that may resist supervision, oversight methods that scale beyond human ability, and AI-feedback training as an RLHF successor. All three papers sit squarely in the Anthropic-empirical and Redwood Research traditions, and the followed-author list reinforces this: control, scalable oversight, deception evals, model organisms, and the governance layer that wraps them (RSPs, frontier safety frameworks, policy). Their current question is governance-grounded — how are RSPs actually being operationalized, and where do dangerous capability evals stand for frontier models — but the substrate is always empirical safety work. They want signal across the full portfolio (scalable oversight, AI control, model organisms of misalignment, deception evaluations, red-teaming, and AI governance and policy) and explicitly filter out pure capabilities benchmarking without safety framing, speculative long-horizon philosophy, and ungrounded ML theory.
current_question: How are leading labs operationalizing their RSPs/safety frameworks in practice? What's the state of dangerous capability evals for current frontier models?
interests: AI control scheming and subversion protocols
AI sabotage detection and safety protocols
AI security and cyber capability evaluation
lab safety commitments and if-then policies

authors.name: Paul Christiano
authors.why: Originator of scalable oversight, debate, and IDA — the conceptual foundations underlying all three seed papers; continues publishing on oversight and control.

authors.name: Jan Leike
authors.why: Heads Anthropic's alignment science team; works directly on scalable oversight, weak-to-strong generalization, and robustness — overlaps all three seed topics.

authors.name: Beth Barnes
authors.why: Founded METR; leads dangerous capability evaluations and model-organism work that directly addresses the user's current question on evals.

authors.name: Ajeya Cotra
authors.why: Former lead on OP AI safety grantmaking, now at METR; known for timelines analysis and thinking about RSP-style safety commitments and crunch-time dynamics.

authors.name: Yoshua Bengio
authors.why: Chairs the International AI Safety Report 2026; bridges technical AI safety and high-level policy — key signal for the governance dimension.

authors.name: Dan Hendrycks
authors.why: Directs CAIS; publishes safety benchmarks and evaluations that bridge empirical safety research and governance advocacy.

authors.name: Buck Shlegeris
authors.why: Co-originated the AI control research agenda (seed paper #1); central figure on scheming threat models and control evaluation design.

authors.name: Ryan Greenblatt
authors.why: Lead author on the AI Control paper (seed paper #1); developing control evaluations for scheming and research-sabotage threat models.

authors.name: Holden Karnofsky
authors.affiliation: Anthropic (technical staff, RSP and safety planning)
authors.why: Works on Anthropic's RSPs and safety planning; Cold Takes blog bridges safety strategy and governance practice at the frontier.

authors.name: Helen Toner
authors.why: Leads CSET; expert on geopolitics of AI, frontier AI governance, and international safety frameworks — core to the user's policy coverage.

authors.name: Evan Hubinger
authors.why: Key theorist of deceptive alignment and model organisms of misalignment; provides theoretical foundations for the deception eval agenda the user tracks.

authors.name: Sam Bowman
authors.why: Lead author on the Scalable Oversight paper (seed paper #2); driving the empirical program for measuring scalable oversight progress.

authors.name: Fabien Roger
authors.why: Co-author on the AI Control paper (seed paper #1); works on control evaluation methodology, trusted monitoring, and anti-collusion protocols.

authors.name: Kshitij Sachan
authors.why: Co-author on the AI Control paper (seed paper #1); works on untrusted monitoring and multi-instance verification in control protocols.

authors.name: Victoria Krakovna
authors.why: Leads specification gaming and reward hacking research at DeepMind — practical failure modes directly relevant to scalable oversight and control.

authors.name: Jacob Steinhardt
authors.why: Publishes broadly on robustness, distributional shift, and emergent capabilities; empirical safety work that spans the user's eval and oversight interests.

authors.name: Deep Ganguli
authors.why: Leads red-teaming and evaluations research at Anthropic; directly relevant to the adversarial evaluation dimension of the user's portfolio.

authors.name: Owain Evans
authors.why: Works on situational awareness, deceptive alignment, and model beliefs — empirical angle on model organisms and deception evals.

sources.name: METR Blog
sources.why: Primary source for dangerous capability evaluations and autonomy evals — posts from May 2026 include reviews of Anthropic's risk reports and RSP operationalization; directly answers the current question.

sources.name: METR Evaluation Reports
sources.why: Index of METR's per-model dangerous capability evaluation reports (o3, o4-mini, DeepSeek-R1, Claude 3.7 etc.) — the most current empirical state of the eval art.

sources.name: Anthropic Alignment Science
sources.why: Active monthly blog (multiple posts per month in 2026) covering scalable oversight, AI control, model organisms, and weak-to-strong generalization — core to all three seed papers.

sources.name: Redwood Research Blog
sources.why: Primary outlet for AI control and scheming research by Shlegeris, Greenblatt et al.; posts in 2026 cover deployment-time misalignment and research sabotage in ML codebases.

sources.name: Apollo Research Blog
sources.why: Confirmed active through May 2026; the go-to lab for empirical deception evaluations and in-context scheming research — model organisms agenda in practice.

sources.name: Anthropic Research
sources.why: Covers Constitutional AI successors, RSP updates, alignment science, and policy — broad primary source across all three seed topics and the governance angle.

sources.name: Anthropic Responsible Scaling Policy
sources.why: Canonical primary source for RSP operationalization; v3.0 published February 2026 with Frontier Safety Roadmaps and public Risk Reports every 3–6 months.

sources.name: UK AI Safety Institute (AISI)
sources.why: Confirmed active through May 2026; publishes independent dangerous capability evals (cyber, autonomy) and frontier model assessments separate from the labs themselves.

sources.name: FAR.AI
sources.why: Active in 2026 with upcoming events; portfolio covers robustness, deception, red-teaming, and model evaluation — complements the control and eval work in the user's brief.

sources.name: Center for AI Safety
sources.why: Dan Hendrycks-led org publishing technical safety benchmarks and multi-disciplinary safety research bridging empirical evals and broader governance advocacy.

sources.name: Georgetown CSET
sources.why: Annual report through 2026; premier policy research on frontier AI governance, export controls, and CyberAI — matches the governance and security dimension of the user's portfolio.

sources.name: Google DeepMind Research
sources.why: Active through April 2026; source for specification gaming, reward hacking, and scalable oversight work from Krakovna, Shah, and the DeepMind safety team.

filter_outs: Pure capabilities work with no safety angle
Speculative long-term alignment philosophy with no near-term connection
notes: All three seed papers come from the Anthropic-empirical and Redwood Research traditions and cluster around a specific program: building oversight and control mechanisms that remain reliable as models become more capable or actively subversive. The user's current question about RSP operationalization and dangerous capability evals adds a governance layer that is already present in their followed-author list (Toner, Karnofsky, Bengio). Sources were verified live: METR, Anthropic Alignment Science, Apollo Research, Redwood, AISI, and FAR.AI all confirmed posts in 2025–2026 by direct page fetch. The author list was expanded from 10 to 18 by surfacing frequent seed-paper coauthors (Sachan, Roger, Bowman), the deceptive alignment adjacent cluster (Hubinger, Evans), the empirical robustness cluster (Steinhardt, Krakovna), and Anthropic red-teaming leads (Ganguli) — all traceable to the exact sub-areas the seeds and freeform identify.
